turns.content: I'm trying to build a Rust tool that automates repetitive tasks. Should I just use std::panic::set_hook for capturing backtraces?

Good question! Using `std::panic::set_hook` is one way to capture backtraces, but it's worth considering whether that fits your needs fully. Sometimes, relying solely on panics can be overkill for more graceful error handling.
But I want the tool to fail gracefully if something goes wrong. So is `std::panic::set_hook` enough?

Actually, just using panic hooks won't give you a user-friendly error message by default; it will still terminate your program abruptly. For better UX and debugging, I recommend combining panic hooks with the `human-panic` crate to provide more readable errors.
Oh right, so I should use both then?

Yes! First, set up a panic hook with `std::panic::set_hook` to capture backtraces. Then integrate the `human-panic` crate for nicer output. This way, you get detailed debugging information plus more friendly error messages that can be customized.
